ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry exists specifically for Beijing-style braised sea fish with tofu. FOODON_03302051 (braised fish food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for braised sea fish with tofu. A0CKP (Fish, cooked) is the closest defensible parent. The tofu component is not captured by this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226529007 (Fish dish) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for Beijing-style braised sea fish with tofu.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Beijing Style Braised Sea Fish with Tofu is approximately 350 g, representing one shared portion of fish and tofu in braising sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Fish, braised, with tofu — composite estimate based on USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked sea fish (FDC ID 175168, Pacific cod, cooked) and firm tofu (FDC ID 172475), combined in approximate 60:40 fish-to-tofu ratio with added soy sauce and oil
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Beijing-style braised sea fish with tofu exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central using a composite of cooked sea fish (Pacific cod as representative lean white sea fish, FDC ID 175168) and firm tofu (FDC ID 172475) in a 60:40 ratio by weight, with adjustments for soy sauce (~15 g per 100 g dish) and cooking oil (~5 g per 100 g dish) contributions to sodium and fat. Energy calculated using Atwater factors. This is a medium-confidence estimate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — tofu and fish-based mixed dishes; analogous estimate using firm tofu GI (~15) and white fish GI (~0, negligible carbohydrate) weighted by carbohydrate contribution
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Beijing-style braised sea fish with tofu. GI estimated at 32 based on the dominant carbohydrate sources: firm tofu (GI ~15, University of Sydney GI Database) and minor contributions from soy sauce and sugar. The dish is low in total carbohydrate (3.8 g per 100 g), so the glycemic load per 350 g serving is approximately 1.3 g, which is very low. GI category is low. Estimation basis: carbohydrate-weighted average of tofu GI and sauce-derived sugars.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and braising liquid
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sodium preparation, as the estimated sodium content of approximately 480 mg per 100 g (approximately 1680 mg per 350 g serving) represents a substantial proportion of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions, as it is low in carbohydrate (3.8 g per 100 g) with a low glycemic index (~32) and provides high-quality protein from fish and tofu that supports satiety without causing rapid postprandial glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from sea fish offset by high sodium from soy sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The sea fish component provides cardioprotective omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids and lean protein, which are beneficial for cardiovascular health; however, the high sodium content from soy sauce-based braising liquid warrants caution for individuals with cardiovascular disease, and reduced-sodium soy sauce or smaller portions are advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beijing Style Braised Sea Fish with Tofu (京味海鱼烧豆腐, Jīng wèi hǎiyú shāo dòufu) is a classic Northern Chinese dish originating from Beijing culinary tradition. It features whole or large pieces of sea fish — typically a lean white-fleshed variety such as sea bass, grouper, or cod — braised together with firm tofu in a richly seasoned sauce made from soy sauce, Shaoxing rice wine, ginger, garlic, scallions, a small amount of sugar, and sesame oil. The fish is often lightly pan-seared before braising to develop a golden crust and prevent the flesh from breaking apart during the slow-cooking process. The resulting dish is savory, umami-rich, and mildly sweet, with tender fish that absorbs the aromatic braising liquid and silken-to-firm tofu that soaks up the sauce. The texture contrast between the flaky fish and the soft tofu is a defining characteristic. Nutritionally, this dish is a high-protein, moderate-fat, low-carbohydrate preparation. The sea fish contributes lean protein, omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids, vitamin B12, selenium, and vitamin D. The tofu adds plant-based protein, calcium, iron, and isoflavones. The primary nutritional concern is the sodium content, which is elevated due to the soy sauce-based braising liquid. The glycemic index is low (~32), making it appropriate for individuals managing blood glucose. It is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management in moderate portions. Individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease should be mindful of sodium intake. The dish is halal-compliant and dairy-free. It is commonly served as a shared main course in Chinese family-style dining, accompanied by steamed rice.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Beijing-style braised sea fish with tofu was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central using a composite model of cooked sea fish and firm tofu with soy sauce and oil adjustments. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for tofu and the negligible glycemic contribution of white fish. Confidence is medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central (cooked Pacific cod FDC ID 175168 and firm tofu FDC ID 172475) in an approximate 60:40 fish-to-tofu ratio with soy sauce and oil adjustments. No direct laboratory analysis or approved regional database entry exists for this specific dish. 2. Sodium estimate (480 mg per 100 g) reflects typical soy sauce-based braising; actual sodium may vary significantly depending on the amount of soy sauce used and whether reduced-sodium variants are employed. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this dish uses plant-based cooking oil and fish, neither of which contains industrially produced trans fats; naturally zero by ingredient composition. 4. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. Ontology codes use closest_parent matches as no exact codes exist for this specific dish in FoodOn, FoodEx2, or SNOMED CT. 6. The dish is served at See Ya Tonight 今晚有局 restaurant in Singapore at SGD 62.40, indicating a premium restaurant preparation. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite and are not individually confirmed by a single approved source entry for this dish.

improvement_actions_taken: Constructed full multilingual local_names including Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi to satisfy G6 and Q1 for Chinese cuisine served in Singapore context.
Built composite nutrient estimate from USDA FoodData Central FDC IDs 175168 and 172475 with soy sauce and oil adjustments as no direct approved regional database entry exists.
Estimated GI at 32 using University of Sydney GI Database analogous values for tofu and documented the estimation basis.
Selected closest_parent ontology codes for FoodOn, FoodEx2, and SNOMED CT with gap notes; used only permitted match_type values.
Documented trans_fat_g = 0.0 as naturally zero in notes to satisfy G4 zero-macro justification rule.
Verified all aliases_by_language entries appear verbatim in flat aliases list to satisfy Q8 consistency rule.
